medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antiviral is the definitive treatment for herpes zoster and is most effective started within 72 hours of rash onset — the rash appeared today. It shortens the illness and reduces the risk of postherpetic neuralgia. Because it requires a prescription and the diagnosis needs in-person confirmation, the telehealth AI Doctor's role is to recognise zoster and arrange same-day in-person assessment for prompt initiation, not to prescribe it remotely. Dose taken verbatim from the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: valaciclovir 1000 mg orally three times daily for 7 days

medications.indication_rationale: Zoster-associated pain is neuropathic and responds poorly to simple analgesia; a gabapentinoid targets the nerve pain and may reduce the burden of postherpetic neuralgia. Requires a prescription and dose review, so it belongs to the same-day in-person prescriber. Dose taken verbatim from the source note; no dose was synthesised.
medications.dose_route_frequency: pregabalin 75 mg orally at night (titrated as directed by the prescriber)
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive sedation/dizziness with other CNS depressants — relevant to fall risk in an older adult; the in-person prescriber should review the full medication list.

medications.indication_rationale: Regular paracetamol provides background analgesia alongside the neuropathic agent. The patient already takes it without relief, so it is an adjunct, not the primary pain treatment for neuropathic zoster pain.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally four times daily (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.drug_name: topical antibiotic cream or occlusive/adhesive dressing applied to the rash
medications.indication_rationale: The lesions should be left clean, dry and uncovered to speed crusting; sticky/occlusive dressings and topical creams trap moisture and can promote secondary bacterial infection. Recommending them is an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Occlusive dressings retain moisture and impair crusting; adhesive dressings traumatise fragile vesicles.

medications.indication_rationale: This is a viral dermatomal eruption, not bacterial cellulitis; empirical antibiotics do not treat zoster and reflect misdiagnosis of the vesicular rash as a spreading soft-tissue infection. Reserved only if genuine bacterial superinfection later develops.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the rash clean, dry and uncovered; do not apply sticky dressings or creams. Avoid scratching or de-roofing the blisters.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Leaving lesions dry speeds crusting and reduces the risk of secondary bacterial infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take regular deep breaths despite the chest-wall pain (once analgesia is working), rather than persistently shallow-breathing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pain-limited shallow breathing predisposes to atelectasis and chest infection; adequate analgesia enables deeper breathing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Until all blisters have crusted over, avoid close contact with pregnant women, unvaccinated infants, and anyone with a weakened immune system.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Vesicle fluid contains varicella-zoster virus and can cause chickenpox in a non-immune contact.

patient_education_points.point: This is the chickenpox virus from childhood reactivating in a single nerve, which is why the pain is on one side in a band and why it burns like an electric shock. Ordinary painkillers often do not switch off this nerve pain, so a specific nerve-pain medicine is used.
Antiviral medicine works best when started within about three days of the rash appearing, so it is important to be seen in person today to confirm the diagnosis and start treatment.
The nerve pain can last for weeks or sometimes months after the rash heals (postherpetic neuralgia) — this is common and there are treatments for it.
The blisters will turn cloudy, then crust over the next 7-10 days; keep the area clean, dry and uncovered, and stay away from pregnant women and young babies until it has fully crusted.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 3-5 days after the same-day in-person assessment and treatment start
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control and the need to titrate neuropathic analgesia, healing/crusting of the vesicles, any signs of secondary infection, and whether postherpetic neuralgia is developing
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Uncontrolled pain despite medication, the rash spreading or new blisters beyond the one band, any eye symptoms, or new breathlessness/productive cough

must_include_items: Recognise a dermatomal vesicular eruption consistent with shingles and communicate it in lay terms
Arrange same-day in-person assessment (urgent GP / urgent care) so a systemic antiviral can be started within the 72-hour window and the diagnosis confirmed
Keep atypical cardiac chest pain on the differential and use the inability to examine/ECG in an elderly patient to justify in-person (not remote) assessment
Advise keeping the rash clean, dry and uncovered, and infection-control precautions around pregnant women and infants
Safety-net for cardiac features, disseminated rash, eye involvement, and breathlessness
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day telehealth-video review by a prescriber who can then arrange in-person examination is acceptable provided in-person assessment and antiviral initiation occur the same day
Naming the likely condition as shingles or a reactivation of the chickenpox virus are equivalent acceptable framings
errors_of_omission: Not arranging any in-person assessment (remote reassurance only)
Not recognising the antiviral treatment window
Failing to screen for eye involvement
No safety-netting for the cardiac mimic or for dissemination
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging uncomplicated thoracic shingles to an emergency ambulance (000)
Prescribing or advising a topical antibiotic cream or occlusive/adhesive dressing on the rash
Treating the rash as bacterial cellulitis with oral antibiotics
Dismissing the chest pain as skin-only and reassuring remotely without in-person assessment in an elderly patient
Synthesising a paediatric or unsupported antiviral dose
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising the dermatomal vesicular rash as shingles and (2) arranging same-day in-person assessment for antiviral initiation while retaining the cardiac mimic on the differential. Remote reassurance that misses the window or the cardiac mimic is under-triage; escalating uncomplicated thoracic zoster to 000 is over-triage. Either is a defect.
